coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots and C-doping porous BN nanoribbon heterojunctions for boosting CO2 photoreduction
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Zhao
Cai, Huayi
Zhao, Zhiyuan
Guo, Zhonglu
Lin, Jing
Huang, Yang
Tang, Chengchun
Chen, Guifeng
Fang, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a photosensitizer have been introduced on the surface of C-doping porous BN nanoribbons (C-BNNR) to form a series of 0D/1D GQDs/C-BNNR heterojunctions as metal-free catalysts with excellent photocatalytic activity. the C-BNNR with sensitization of GQDs is innovatively applied to the field of CO2 photocatalytic reduction. The results reveal that GQDs/C-BNNR heterojunctions exhibit excellent optical properties and photocatalytic activity. The CO production rate of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 (33.47 μmol g-1h-1) is 14.49, 4.60 and 2.95 times higher than GQDs (2.31 μmol g-1h-1), BNNR (7.27 μmol g-1h-1) and C-BNNR (11.34 μmol g-1h-1), respectively. This work demonstrates that the addition of GQDs can significantly improve the light utilization of C-BNNR without any effect on the structure. In addition, the strong interaction between the GQDs and C-BNNR leads to the formation of a built-in electric field. It allows photogenerated electrons in GQDs to be transferred into C-BNNR and complexed with holes. As a result, the photogenerated electrons in C-BNNR are induced to participate in the CO2 photocatalytic reduction process. Meanwhile, GQDs/C-BNNR possess certain CO2 photocatalytic reduction ability in the NIR region due to the upconversion ability of GQDs. This work pioneer the application of GQDs/C-BNNR heterojunctions in CO2 photocatalytic reduction and further provides new ideas for the design of novel metal-free catalysts.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots and C-doping porous BN nanoribbon heterojunctions for boosting CO2 photoreduction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a photosensitizer have been introduced on the surface of C-doping porous BN nanoribbons (C-BNNR) to form a series of 0D/1D GQDs/C-BNNR heterojunctions as metal-free catalysts with excellent photocatalytic activity. the C-BNNR with sensitization of GQDs is innovatively applied to the field of CO2 photocatalytic reduction. The results reveal that GQDs/C-BNNR heterojunctions exhibit excellent optical properties and photocatalytic activity. The CO production rate of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 (33.47 μmol g-1h-1) is 14.49, 4.60 and 2.95 times higher than GQDs (2.31 μmol g-1h-1), BNNR (7.27 μmol g-1h-1) and C-BNNR (11.34 μmol g-1h-1), respectively. This work demonstrates that the addition of GQDs can significantly improve the light utilization of C-BNNR without any effect on the structure. In addition, the strong interaction between the GQDs and C-BNNR leads to the formation of a built-in electric field. It allows photogenerated electrons in GQDs to be transferred into C-BNNR and complexed with holes. As a result, the photogenerated electrons in C-BNNR are induced to participate in the CO2 photocatalytic reduction process. Meanwhile, GQDs/C-BNNR possess certain CO2 photocatalytic reduction ability in the NIR region due to the upconversion ability of GQDs. This work pioneer the application of GQDs/C-BNNR heterojunctions in CO2 photocatalytic reduction and further provides new ideas for the design of novel metal-free catalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing industrialization process is inseparable from the massive use of fossil fuels. With the significant increase in CO2 and other greenhouse gas emissions, the carbon balance of nature has been disrupted, leading to an increasing greenhouse effect[1–3]. Therefore, exploring a sustainable economy, which converts CO2 into other chemical raw materials (CH3OH, HCOOH, etc.) or high value-added fuels (CO, CH4, etc.), to ameliorate the global energy crisis and environmental pollution has become a global goal and challenge[4,5]. Inspired by photosynthesis in nature, photocatalytic CO2 reduction (PCR), which converts solar energy into chemical energy using water and CO2 as raw materials, is regarded as one of the effective methods to deal with the above problems[6–9]. As the most important part of the PCR, a variety of photocatalysts with CO2 reduction ability have been designed. It includes precious metals (Au, Pt, etc.), metal oxides (TiO2, ZnO, etc.), Bi-based semiconductors (Bi2MoO6, BiOBr, etc.), metal-organic frameworks (ZIF-8, UIO-66, etc.), etc. However, the high price of metal-based catalysts is not conducive to large-scale applications from a sustainability perspective[10–12]. Furthermore, with the deepening of the investigation, researchers detect that photocatalysts with excellent performance require efficient carrier separation rates and excellent adsorption capacity. Therefore, it is particularly important to develop metal-free catalysts with resourceful, low-cost, eco-friendly, high-efficiency and strong adsorption capacity[13,14].

Hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN), a typical metal-free material, facilitates the qualitative transfer of electrons due to the B-N covalent bond with ionic properties. Therefore, compared with other metal-free catalysts, h-BN has unique strengths in the field of photocatalysis[15–17]. However, the large bandgap (about 5.6 eV) leads to the weak light utilization of h-BN, which quite limits its research and development in the field of photocatalysis[18]. Fortunately, porous BN, as a type of h-BN with an amorphous structure, retains the unique strengths of h-BN while having a larger specific surface area. Thus, it possesses more active sites for adsorption and reaction[19]. At present, a few reports have confirmed that porous BN has CO2 photocatalytic reduction ability. However, the photocatalytic activity of porous BN is still poor compared with other materials. The main reason for this phenomenon is that although the bandgap of porous BN has decreased, the light utilization and photo-generated carrier separation rate are still not satisfactory. Therefore, to achieve the efficient photocatalytic reduction of CO2 by porous BN materials, it is necessary to further expand the light absorption range and photo-generated carrier separation rate without destroying the high specific surface area structure. Benefiting from the advantages of easily modifiable microstructure and surface properties of porous BN, researchers have explored various methods to ameliorate the key issues, including elemental doping[20,21] and constructing heterojunctions. For example, Cao et al[22]. demonstrated that the introduction of O elements in porous BN can improve the light absorption capacity, and the B-O bonds can improve the CO2 adsorption capacity. Lu et al[23]. prepared C-doping porous BN with different structures. The C-doping narrowed the bandgap of porous BN and promoted the separation of photogenerated electron holes. Therefore, Both of the two catalysts exhibit high photocatalytic activity, and the CO yields are 63.1 and 42.6 μmol g-1 h-1, respectively. Du et al[24]. introduced Ti3C2Tx into p-BN, and the Schottky heterojunctions can effectively inhibit the complexation of carriers in p-BN. The porous BN has many different morphologies, such as BN nanosheets, BN nanofibers, BN nanoribbons and BN nanospheres[25]. Compared with BN nanofibers, BN nanoribbons have a larger width, which can provide more active sites. Meanwhile, BN nanoribbons possess a higher aspect ratio than BN nanosheets. It means that BN nanoribbons have stronger directional transfer ability of photogenerated carriers. Liu et al[26]. mentioned that BN nanoribbons contain abundant surface functional groups and large spatial structures, hence exhibiting excellent adsorption properties. Li et al[27]. demonstrated that BN nanoribbons reveal excellent N2 trapping ability due to the existence of edge lone pair electrons.

In recent years, carbon nanomaterials have attracted extensive attention due to their unique physicochemical properties. Thereinto, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a graphene-derived material with a minor diameter (<10 nm), possess certain semiconductor properties because of the quantum confinement effect[28–30]. Furthermore, the high chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, unique upconversion ability and abundant edge site density of GQDs indicate it is an ideal photosensitizer[31–33]. Currently, researchers have used GQDs to modify other photocatalysts and achieved excellent results. Liu et al[34]. demonstrated that the interaction between GQDs and MIL-101(Fe) can lead to the rapid separation of photo-induced carriers, resulting in GQDs/MIL-101(Fe) exhibiting excellent photocatalytic performance. Xiong et al[35]. synthesized Bi2WO6 co-modified with surface oxygen vacancies (VO) and GQDs, and the CO yield (43.9 μmol g-1 h-1) under visible light is 1.7 times than Bi2WO6.

Herein, it is considered that the conjugate structure similar to h-BN and GQDs is conducive to form the heterojunction. Therefore, we successfully synthesized novel 0D/1D GQDs/C-BNNR heterojunctions by compounding one-dimensional (1D) C-doping porous BN nanoribbons (C-BNNR) with zero-dimensional (0D) GQDs via a simple physical mixing method, and innovatively applied in CO2 photocatalytic reduction. The introduction of GQDs plays a crucial role in inhibiting the complexation of photogenerated carriers in C-BNNR while improving the light absorption capacity. The structure, morphology, CO2 photocatalytic reduction ability and photoelectrochemical properties of the catalyst were analyzed through experiments. Furthermore, the effect of GQDs on photocatalytic activity was revealed. Finally, the possible electron transfer mechanism and the whole CO2 photocatalytic reduction process were also deduced. This work further investigates the application of porous BN materials in CO2 photocatalytic reduction and guides the design of more novel metal-free catalysts.

Boric acid (H3BO3, 99.9 %, Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., ltd), Melamine (C3N6H6, 99.9 %, Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd), Glucose (C6H12O6, 99.9 %, Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., ltd), Citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.9 %, Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., ltd), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 97.0 %, Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., ltd).

In a typical experiment[23], a certain amount of boric acid, melamine and glucose (molar ratio = 6:2:1) were dispersed into 500 mL of pure water and stirred continuously until a white suspension was formed. Then, the above suspension was heated to 90 °C with stirring conditions. During the heating process, the white substance gradually dissolved and the solution became pink and clear. After being heated at 90 °C for 3 h, the solution was quickly poured into liquid nitrogen to cool, and then it was vacuum freeze-dried to obtain the fluffy pink precursor. Finally, the precursor was calcined at 1100 °C under N2 for 4 h to gain grey C-BNNR.
The synthesis of BNNR is the same as C-BNNR, except that glucose is not added to the preparation of the precursor.

GQDs were obtained via a simple cracking method of citric acid. The specific operation was as follows: A container containing 2 g of citric acid was heated at 200 °C for 20 min. It was observed that citric acid gradually changed from white powder to brown liquid throughout the heating process. After that, the brown liquid was added to 100 mL NaOH solution (0.5 M). The red-brown GQDs solution was obtained after 30 min of vigorous stirring. Then, the GQDs solution was dialyzed in pure water for 24 h, and the pure water was changed every 12 h. The dialyzed GQDs solution was dried at 70 °C to obtain GQDs powder, then redispersed into a certain amount of ultrapure water to gain the GQDs solution with 2 mg mL-1.

The preparation process of the GQDs/C-BNNR heterojunctions is shown in Fig. 1 . 0.1 g C-BNNR was dispersed in ethanol aqueous solution (V(H2O):V(C2H5OH) = 2:1) with ultrasonic for 1 h. Then, different volumes of GQDs solution were dripped into the above solution and stirred for 12 h. Finally, a series of GQDs/C-BNNR heterojunctionswere obtained when the solution was centrifuged and the precipitation was washed and dried. According to the volume of the GQDs solution, the composites were named GQDs/C-BNNR-1, 2, 3, 4 and 5.

The physical phase composition and crystal structure of the catalyst were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8-advance, Bruker, 40 kV, Cu Kα radiation source). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, VECTOR22, 4000-400 cm−1) was used to analyze the chemical bonds and surface functional groups of the samples. The composition and chemical structure of materials were analyzed by Raman Spectrograph at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm (Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution). The content of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen was recorded on LECO TC-500. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi) was utilized to measure the elemental composition and chemical state of the catalyst. The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of samples were collected by Automatic surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics ASAP 2460). The surface area, pore size distribution and pore volume were calculated and analyzed by the BET method. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Quanta 450FEG) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200S) were applied to characterize the microstructure of the materials. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were identified using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (U-3900H, 240–800 nm) to analyze the light absorption properties and band gap of the sample. In situ FTIR experiments were performed on the Bruker INVENIO R FT-IR spectrometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra and fluorescence lifetime of catalysts were obtained from fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500) and Steadystate transient fluorescence spectrometer (Fluorolog-3 Jobin-Yvon spectrophotometer), respectively.

The CO2 photocatalytic reduction capacity of the samples was measured under UV light irradiation, where the reaction was carried out in a quartz reactor (BL-500, Shanghai LABE Instrument Co., ltd., China) with a volume of 200 mL. The reaction was performed as follows: 20 mg of catalyst was deposited in the bottom of the reactor with magnets and added with 5 mL of pure water. Afterward, the reactor was continuously filled with high-purity CO2 gas in a dark environment and irradiated with a 300 W Xe lamp (λ = 200–420 nm or λ > 700 nm) for 4 h. Then, the reactor was fed with condensate and the suspension of catalysts was stirred with a magnetic stirrer. After the reaction was completed, the gas and liquid in the reactor was collected by a syringe. The gaseous and liquid products generated and contents were examined in a gas chromatograph equipped with barrier ionization discharge (BID) detector and flame ionization detector (FID) and thermal conductivity detector (TCD), respectively. In addition, the selectivity of the catalyst for CO and CH4 was calculated by Eq. (1) and (2) [24]:

Mott-Schottky curves, photocurrent curves, and electrochemical impedance curves of catalysts were measured at the CHI670 electrochemical workstation using a three-electrode system. The ITO glass coated with catalysts, Pt electrode and Ag/AgCl electrode is the working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The working electrode was prepared as follows: Firstly, 10 mg of catalyst and PVDF were mixed in a mortar, and then a certain amount of anhydrous ethanol was added to it and ground continuously. The viscous composite was evenly coated on 2 × 3 cm ITO glass and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h. All the above tests were carried out in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution.

A series of characterizations of GQDs prepared via citric acid cleavage were performed with different test methods. The XRD pattern of GQDs (Fig. 2 (a)) exhibits a weak broad peak centered around 25.6°, which belongs to the (002) crystal plane. Meanwhile, the d-spacing value corresponding to the (002) of GQDs (0.347 nm) is slightly larger than graphene (0.337 nm). This is due to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GQDs[36]. Fig. 2(b) and the inset show TEM and HRTEM images of GQDs, respectively. It can be clearly observed that GQDs reveal a uniformly distributed spherical nanoparticle-like structure and the d-spacing value (inset) is about 0.35 nm, corresponding to the (002), which is similar to the analysis results of XRD. The particle size distribution of GQDs was fitted by the Gaussian function, and the results are shown in Fig. 2(c). The average size of GQDs is about 5.57 nm, because the fitted curve has a narrow half-peak width, the particle size distribution of GQDs is more uniform. Moreover, Raman spectroscopy, as a technique often applied to characterize carbon materials, can analyze important information such as the crystallinity, degree of hybridization and defects of the material from the results. Fig. 2(d) is the Raman spectrum of GQDs in which peaks appear at 1356 and 1584 cm-1, corresponding to the D-band and G-band for GQDs, respectively[37]. The presence of the D-band indicates that the GQDs contain defective structures, which can function as trap states to trap photogenerated electrons. And the G-band is generated by the in-plane vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in GQDs. Therefore, GQDs exhibit relatively high electron conductivity[38]. Fig. 2(e) illustrates the PL spectra of the GQDs solution at different excitation wavelengths (λ = 280-420 nm). With the change of excitation wavelength, the position and intensity of the emission peaks of the GQDs solution are changed. It can be observed from the inset that the GQDs solution is a yellowish liquid under fluorescent light, while it can emit bright blue light under UV light (λ = 354 nm) irradiation.

To determine the crystal structures of BNNR, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR, the XRD patterns of samples were assessed. In Fig. 3 (a), BNNR shows two weak broad peaks at 2θ = 25.6 and 42.8°, which correspond to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of h-BN respectively. Meanwhile, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peak is wide, indicating that the presence of defects in BNNR and crystallinity is low. Compared with BNNR, the (002) peak-centered position of C-BNNR shifted slightly to a high angle (25.6° to 25.8°). It means that the successful C-doping of BNNR reduced the d-spacing value slightly (0.347 nm to 0.345 nm), which is the identical result as previously reported[39]. The XRD patterns of GQDs/C-BNNR are similar to C-BNNR, demonstrating that GQDs cannot affect the crystal structure of C-BNNR. However, due to the low content of GQDs, the characteristic diffraction peak of GQDs cannot be obviously observed. Interestingly, from the local enlargement of the XRD pattern (Fig. 3(b)), it can detect that the diffraction peak position of (002) in the GQDs/C-BNNR changes with the increase of the GQDs content (gradually shifting from 25.8° to 26.2°). This phenomenon demonstrates the interlayer distance of C-BNNR can be affected by enhanced interaction between GQDs and C-BNNR, and ultimately lead to the decrease of the d-spacing value[40].

The composition, chemical structure, surface functional groups and other information of samples can be analyzed intuitively according to the FT-IR. Fig. 4 (a) illustrates the FT-IR spectra of GQDs, BNNR, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3. The absorption band present at around 3420 cm- 1 is assigned to OH stretching vibrations, indicating that –OH is suspended on the surface of all samples. For GQDs, the absorption bands near 2942 and 687 cm- 1 both correspond to CH bonds. Moreover, the absorption bands at 1675, 1567, and 1409 cm- 1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of the CO bond and the symmetric/asymmetric stretching vibrations of the OCO bond[41]. BNNR, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 show NH bond at 3258 cm- 1, demonstrating the presence of –NH2 on the surface. This is due to the low pyrolysis temperature during sample preparation (<1500 °C), resulting in some residual –NH2 from the precursor. Moreover, the peak at 1386 cm-1 can be assigned to the interlayer B-N-B bending vibration of h-BN. Compared with BNNR, the absorption bands caused by symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of − CH2 and − CH3 appear at 2852 and 2921 cm- 1 of C-BNNR (Fig. 4(b) left). Furthermore, the peak resulting in the stretching vibration of the B-N bond within the BNNR is located at 804 cm- 1 (Fig. 4(b) right), while the C-BNNR appears shifted (793 cm- 1). It originates from the disruption of the original conjugate structure with the introduction of the C element[42]. The absorption peaks of BNNR at 985, 1078 and 1166 cm- 1 are derived from the vibration of BOH, B-O and B-OH bonds, respectively. However, the intensity of the peak corresponding to C-BNNR is weaker, indicating that C-doping reduced the number of oxygen-containing functional groups. Interestingly, the absorption peak strength of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 is stronger than C-BNNR in the above range. It means that the introduction of GQDs increases the number of oxygen-containing functional groups. In conclusion, the results further demonstrate the successful preparation of BNNR, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR.

To further verify the constitute and chemical structure of BNNR, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR, Raman spectra of samples were measured (Fig. 5 (a)). For BNNR, the Raman spectrum only reveals a peak at 1372 cm- 1, which corresponds to the E
2g mode of h-BN[43]. However, two characteristic absorption bands can be observed in the Raman spectrum of C-BNNR. The wide peak at about 1300 cm- 1 is attributed to the overlap between the D-band and E
2g mode of carbon material and h-BN, respectively. The peak around 1586 cm- 1 is identified as the G-band of carbon material. The above results demonstrate that C-BNNR retains the structure of h-BN while introducing C atoms. Although the Raman spectrum of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 is similar to C-BNNR, the positions of both Raman peaks exhibited blueshift, while the FWHM is slightly reduced. This phenomenon is attributed to the strong interaction between C-BNNR and GQDs resulting in the charge transfer[44]. Meanwhile, the I(D)/I(G) value of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 (1.04) is slightly less than C-BNNR (1.27), indicating that fewer defect structures are present in GQDs/C-BNNR. Fig. 5(b) reveals the O and C contents of BNNR, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3. The C content of C-BNNR (5.96 %) is significantly more than BNNR (2.16 %). It evidently indicates the successful introduction of the C element. Compared with C-BNNR, GQDs/C-BNNR-3 exhibits an increase in the content of C (6.32 %) and O (9.23 %). It is known from the previous section that a large amount of oxygen-containing functional groups are present in the GQDs.

The XPS energy spectra of C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 were examined and analyzed to investigate the elemental composition and the interaction between GQDs and C-BNNR. Fig. 6 (a) illustrates the survey XPS spectra of C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 corrected by C element (284.8 eV). The result reveals that both samples mainly contained only four elements (B, N, C and O), and the intensity of C and O elements in GQDs/C-BNNR-3 is higher than C-BNNR, which laterally reflects the presence of GQDs. For C-BNNR, the B 1s high-resolution energy spectrum (Fig. 6(b)) can be resolved in three peaks (192.1, 190.6 and 189.6 eV) corresponding to the B-O, B-N and B-C bonds, respectively. With the introduction of GQDs, no significant changes are observed in the B 1s of the GQDs/C-BNNR-3, but all peaks exhibit a slight shift (192.2, 190.7, and 189.7 eV). Likewise, Fig. 6(c) shows the high-resolution spectra of N 1s for C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 are similar, and both deconvolution peaks attribute to NC and N-B can be observed. Thereinto, the binding energies of NC and N-B in C-BNNR (398.8 and 398.2 eV) are slightly lower than GQDs/C-BNNR-3 (398.9 and 398.1 eV). The analysis of the O 1s spectra (Fig. 6(d)) exhibits that C-BNNR has the B-O bond at 532.6 eV. However, GQDs/C-BNNR-3 appear three shoulder peaks belonging to B-O, COC, and OC bonds at 532.7, 533.5, and 531.2 eV, respectively. The presence of COC and OC bonds indicates that oxygen-containing functional groups are suspended on the surface of GQDs, which is caused by incomplete carbonization during citric acid pyrolysis. The peaks at 285.8, 284.7 and 283.2 eV for C 1 s of C-BNNR (Fig. 6(e)) are assigned to CN, CC and C-B bonds, respectively. Compared with C-BNNR, the C 1s spectra of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 can be fitted with four Gaussian peaks (289.1, 285.9, 284.6 and 283.3 eV), where the satellite peak at 289.1 eV can be attributed to the CO bond in GQDs and the other peaks represent the CN, CC and C-B bonds, respectively. The increasing strength of the CN bond peaks in the composites indexes the formation of CN bonds between GQDs and C-BNNR. While, the increase in CC bond strength is attributed to the presence of CC bonds in GQDs, demonstrating that GQDs contain significant defective sites. The results of the above analysis reveal the successful introduction of C elements in C-BNNR. Moreover, the binding energies of the characteristic peaks corresponding to the same chemical bonds in both C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 are different. It suggests that the strong interaction between GQDs and C-BNNR leads to the transfer of some electrons from C-BNNR to GQDs after the composite is formed, which causes the difference in electron density. Therefore, an internal electric field directed from C-BNNR to GQDs is formed[30].

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 were conducted in liquid nitrogen environment at 77 K (Fig. 7 (a)) to investigate the influence of GQDs on the surface structure of C-BNNR. The results reveal that the shapes of the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of samples are quite similar. According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) classification criteria, both isotherms are type IV and the presence of H4-type hysteresis loops indicates the presence of micro and mesopores in C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3. Afterward, the pore size distribution curves, cumulative pore volumes and specific surface areas of C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 were calculated with non-native density flooding theory (NLDFT) and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method, the results are shown in Fig. 7(b)-(d), respectively. The pore size distribution intervals of C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 were similar, mainly concentrated between 2 and 4 nm. Furthermore, the pore volume and specific surface area of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 (0.088 cm3 g- 1 and 89.589 m3 g- 1) were slightly smaller than C-BNNR (0.095 cm3 g- 1 and 104.271 m3 g- 1). The results demonstrate that the addition of GQDs does not have a significant impact on the surface structure of C-BNNR, only part of the pore structure in C-BNNR is blocked. However, the composites can still maintain a large specific surface area, which can provide sufficient adsorption sites for the CO2 photocatalytic reduction process.

Fig. 8 (a)-(c) show typical SEM images of BNNR, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3, respectively. From the figures, all materials present visually a distinct band structure. Meanwhile, the diameters of samples are 100–200 nm and the lengths can reach several tens of microns, possessing a high aspect ratio. The porous framework formed by interleaved nanoribbons is conducive to the adsorption of CO2. Moreover, typical TEM images of BNNR, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 are displayed in Fig. 8(d)-(f), respectively. Similar to the results demonstrated in the SEM images, all of the samples exhibit 1D structures with diameters in the range of 100–200 nm. The above results show that neither the C-doping nor the introduction of GQDs has a significant effect on the morphology and structure of BNNR. In addition, there are a large number of pore structures in BNNR can be observed in the TEM, while the pores in C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 appear to be reduced. It is consistent with the results of the previous BET analysis. The uniform distribution of GQDs on the C-BNNR surface (dashed box) can be further observed in the HRTEM image of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 (Fig. 8(g)), and the lattice stripes of both materials are closely interlaced, providing a path for electron transfer between them. The measured C-BNNR lattice spacing in the composite is 0.34 nm, respectively, corresponding to (002) crystal plane, which is similar to the previous XRD calculations. However, the lattice spacing corresponding to the crystalline surface of GQDs (002) in the composite is 0.33 nm, which is slightly smaller than the XRD calculation of pure GQDs (0.347 nm), again proving the existence of strong interactions between C-BNNR and GQDs. Finally, the elemental distribution plots of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 (Fig. 8(h)-(k)) verify the uniform distribution of GQDs on the C-BNNR surface.

The light absorption ability of the catalyst is an important factor affecting its photocatalytic activity. Therefore, the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of GQDs, BNNR, C-BNNR and a series of GQDs/C-BNNR heterojunctions were examined, and the results are shown in Fig. 9 (a). For GQDs, due to the presence of a large number of groups with strong π-π* and n-π* transitions on surfaces, there is a strong light absorption in the full wavelength band[45]. As previously reported, BNNR can barely absorb light in the visible and infrared bands, with its absorption edge at about 320 nm[46]. However, with the successful C-doping, C-BNNR exhibits a notable enhancement of light absorption ability in all wavelength bands, a phenomenon that is consistent with the physical photograph in the inset. The inset shows BNNR as a white powder, while the color of C-BNNR powder is dark gray, so C-BNNR is supposed to have a stronger light absorption ability. Besides, C-BNNR appears a new absorption peak at around 400 nm, which can be attributed to the presence of defects such as vacancies and impurities[47]. With the addition of GQDs, the light absorption capacity of GQDs/C-BNNR heterojunctions is enhanced. The reason for the phenomenon is that GQDs, as electron conductors, can effectively promote the separation of photogenerated electrons-hole in C-BNNR. On the other hand, the addition of GQDs further converts the C-BNNR from dark gray to black (Fig. 9(a) inset). Fig. 9(b) and the inset show the bandgap (Eg) of BNNR, C-BNNR, GQDs/C-BNNR-3 and GQDs calculated using the Kubelka-Munk (KM) method. A further decrease in the bandgap (3.13 eV) is observed for C-BNNR compared to the previously reported p-BN (3.47 eV). The reason is that the electronegativity of C is between B and N. When the C atom is successfully doped into BNNR, the B or N atom will undergo charge transfer phenomena with the C atom, affecting the respective electronic structures[48]. In addition, the bandgap of GQDs is 2.10 eV, which is similar to other reported works. The bandgap of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 (3.07 eV) is further smaller than C-BNNR. It indicates that the strong interaction between GQDs and C-BNNR can affect the electronic properties of the composites, resulting in the change of the bandgap.

Fig. 9(c) illustrates the Mott-Schottky curves of GQDs, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3. This measurement can better determine the band edge position of catalyst and judge the transfer direction of the photogenerated electrons. The slopes of Mott-Schottky curves for GQDs, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 are positive, indicating that catalysts are n-type semiconductors. The flat-band potentials (Efb ) of GQDs, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 were calculated to be -0.57, -0.79 and -0.66 V (concerning Ag/AgCl electrode), which were converted to standard hydrogen electrode potentials of -0.37, -0.59 and -0.46 V, respectively. For n-type semiconductors, the conduction band potential (E
CB ) is 0.2 V lower than Efb , hence the ECB of the above materials are -0.57, -0.79 and -0.66 eV. The more negative E
CB indicates that the catalyst has the ability to reduce CO2 to gases such as CO and CH4. Also, the valence band potentials (EVB ) of GQDs, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 are calculated to 1.53, 2.34 and 2.41 eV, based on the bandgap of the three materials. The VB-XPB spectra of GQDs, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 were measured and the results are shown in Figure S1. It can be observed that the VB-XPS of GQDs, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 are 1.36, 2.15 and 2.25 eV, respectively. Then, the EVB, NHE is calculated according to the Eq. (3):
(3)
E
VB , N H E
= φ +
E
VB , X P S
- 4.44

where φ is the power function of the XPS instrument and is 4.60 eV in this work. Therefore, the EVB, NHE of GQDs, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR-3 are 1.52, 2.31 and 2.41 eV, respectively, and the ECB, NHE are calculated to be -0.58, -0.82 and -0.65 eV, respectively. The band gap positions of the three materials calculated by this method are similar to the results previously calculated using Mott-Schottky curves, further confirming the accuracy of the band gap positions. Since the Fermi energy level (EF ) of the n-type semiconductor is equal to E fb, the E F of GQDs and C-BNNR are -0.37 and -0.59 eV. By comparing the E F of GQDs and C-BNNR, it can be found that electrons in C-BNNR will be transferred to GQDs, while the two materials are compounded, until the Fermi energy levels are aligned[49]. At this point, a built-in electric field is generated between the two materials from the C-BNNR pointing to the GQDs. Therefore, the photogenerated electrons generated in GQDs under illumination are transferred to C-BNNR and combined with the photogenerated holes in C-BNNR under the built-in electric field. Eventually, the photogenerated electrons in C-BNNR are retained and participate in the subsequent CO2 photocatalytic reduction process, achieving the rapid separation of photogenerated carriers. In conclusion, for photocatalysts, both the increase of light absorption capacity, the decrease of the bandgap, and the formation of the built-in electric field provide the possibility of excellent photocatalytic activity.

The CO2 photocatalytic reduction capacity of BNNR, C-BNNR and a series of GQDs/C-BNNR were investigated in the closed system, and the results are shown in Fig. 10 . Under UV light (λ = 200–420 nm) irradiation, a certain amount of gas and liquid in the closed system was extracted at regular intervals, and the gas and liquid composition and content of each component were tested and analyzed using gas chromatography. However, the presence of liquid products was not evident. As shown in Fig. 10(a), all catalysts have a certain CO2 photocatalytic reduction ability and the only gas phase products are CO and a small amount of H2 and CH4. The GQDs have a certain CO2 photocatalytic reduction ability, and its CO and CH4 yield is 2.31 and 0.53 μmol g-1 h- 1. For BNNR and C-BNNR, the photocatalytic activities of both materials are relatively weak, but the CO yield of C-BNNR (11.34 μmol g-1 h- 1) is better than BNNR (7.27 μmol g-1 h- 1). It indicates that further reduction of the bandgap by C-doping can effectively improve the photocatalytic activity of the materials. After loading GQDs on C-BNNR, the CO2 photocatalytic activity of the composites appears to be significantly enhanced, and the CO yield increases with the increase of the GQDs content. Among them, GQDs/C-BNNR-3 exhibited the most excellent photocatalytic ability with a CO yield of 33.47 μmol g-1 h- 1, which was 14.49, 4.60 and 2.95 times than GQDs, BNNR and C-BNNR, respectively. The H2 yield for all catalysts is quite low, which is shown in Figure S2. However, with the further increase of GQDs content, the CO yield of the composites slightly decreased, but still possessed high photocatalytic activity. The reason for this phenomenon is that the excessive amount of GQDs not only hinders the absorption of UV light by C-BNNR due to the shielding effect, but also occupies too many reactive sites on the surface of C-BNNR. Additionally, the CO selectivity of the GQDs/C-BNNR (Fig. 10(b)), especially GQDs/C-BNNR-3 (88.06 %), was also better than GQDs (48.23 %), BNNR (80.51 %) and C-BNNR (83.69 %). The apparent quantum yields (AQY) of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 in UV light (λ = 350 nm) was calculated using Eqs. (4–6)[50]:
(4)

(5)

N A
(6)

I A t λ
hc

where n is the molar amount of CO molecule, NA is Avogadro constant, h is the Planck constant, c is the speed of light, A is the irradiation area, I is the intensity of the irradiation, t is the photoreaction time, and λ is the wavelength of the monochromatic light. The result showed that the AQY of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 is 0.2 %. The products of the whole photocatalytic process include H2/CO/CH4 and O2 from CO2 reduction and H2O oxidation, respectively. Fig. 10(c) is the curves of cumulative CO, CH4 and O2 yields with time for the GQDs/C-BNNR-3. The yields of CO, CH4 and O2 within 4 h were approximately linearly correlated with time, indicating that GQDs/C-BNNR-3 can work stably and persistently during the catalytic proceeding. The yield of O2 is 11.12 μmol g- 1 and less than the stoichiometric ratio of total CH4 and CO, which is similar to the results of the reported work[51]. The source of CO and CH4 was further verified by setting up five sets of control tests with GQDs/C-BNNR-3 as an example (Fig. 10(d)). The results demonstrate that only trace amounts of CO/CH4 were detected in the products without catalyst, CO2, H2O or light. The in suit FT-IR spectra of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 (Figure S3) show that many characteristic peaks can be observed after adsorption. They correspond to water (H2O, 1630 cm−1), carbonate (CO3 2–, 1608 and 1462 cm−1), bicarbonate (HCO3 –, 1423 cm−1), bidentate carbonate (b-CO3 2–, 1358 cm−1) and monodentate carbonate (m-CO3 2–, 1503 and 1298 cm−1), which indicates that H2O and CO2 were successfully adsorbed on the catalyst surface[52–55]. The position of the characteristic peak of H2O in the curve shifted a little with the light (1636 cm−1), while the characteristic peaks of ·CO2 – and HCOO– appeared at 1657 cm−1 and 1624, 1561, and 1518 cm−1. These groups are regarded as intermediates in the CO2 photocatalytic reduction to generate CO[56]. The above phenomenons effectively demonstrate that the products come from the CO2 photocatalytic reduction process in the presence of the catalyst. The main product of CO can be used as the raw gas of the Fischer-Tropsch synthesis reaction to produce other high-carbon chemicals, which have positive significance for industrial and chemical fields. In conclusion, the results demonstrate that the appropriate introduction of GQDs can positively affect the photocatalytic activity of C-BNNR.

Considering that GQDs have upconversion capability, which can emit light at lower wavelengths under the excitation of the NIR region. Therefore, we measured the PL spectra of the GQDs solutions at excitation wavelengths of 700, 800, and 900 nm (Figure S4(a)). The results revealed that the GQDs solution possessed a certain upconversion ability, but the intensity of PL is weak. The CO2 photocatalytic reduction ability of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 in the NIR region was tested (Figure S4(b)). The catalyst can stably reduce CO2 to CO/CH4 and oxidize H2O to O2 in the NIR region. However, compared to UV, the yield of products is lower. The reasons for this appearance are as follows: (1) the GQDs, which were prepared by the citric acid cracking process, have a weak upconversion ability; (2) the content of the GQDs loaded on the C-BNNR is low. However, it is fascinating that the catalyst has the CO2 photocatalytic reduction ability of the catalyst in NIR region, which can provide a new idea for the design of photocatalysts in NIR region.

The CO2 photocatalytic reduction cycle of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 was performed with the same conditions to facilitate a better assessment of its stability. Fig. 11 (a) shows that the CO yield of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 (123.81 μmol g-1) can still be maintained at 92.48 % of the initial yield within 4 h after five cycles. It proves that GQDs/C-BNNR-3 exhibits excellent stability in the CO2 photocatalytic reduction process. The recycled GQDs/C-BNNR-3 were cleaned, centrifuged and dried, then subjected to a series of characterizations. The SEM images (Fig. 11(b)) show that the microstructure of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 after the reaction is the same as before the reaction (Fig. 8(c)), still exhibiting a distinct band structure. Meanwhile, the XRD patterns before and after the reaction of GQDs/C-BNNR-3 (Fig. 11(c)) reveal that the physical phase and crystal structure of the composites have not changed, and only a small increase in crystallinity is observed. The reason for this phenomenon is the hydrolysis of part of the B-O bonds and part of the defective structure in C-BNNR, which is also responsible for the slight decrease in catalyst activity. In addition, the FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 11(d)) results proved that the chemical structure of the GQDs/C-BNNR-3 is not changed. In conclusion, GQDs/C-BNNR have stable catalytic activity and structural stability.

In summary, C-doping 1D porous BN nanoribbons (C-BNNR) with high aspect ratio and specific surface area were synthesized via high-temperature pyrolysis. Then, 0D/1D GQDs/C-BNNR heterojunctions with excellent CO2 photocatalytic activity were constructed by introducing GQDs as photosensitizers. The analysis and measurement results suggest that the C-doping and the introduction of GQDs have no significant effect on the morphology and structure of BNNR. However, compared to BNNR, C-BNNR and GQDs/C-BNNR possess stronger light absorption, smaller bandgap, and superior light utilization. Moreover, due to the formation of the built-in electric field between C-BNNR and GQDs, the photogenerated electrons in GQDs will combine with the photogenerated holes in C-BNNR, so that more photogenerated electrons in C-BNNR will participate in the CO2 photocatalytic reduction process. Finally, the rapid separation of photogenerated carriers is achieved. Consequently, the CO yield of the best performing GQDs/C-BNNR-3 under light (33.47 μmol g-1 h- 1) was 14.49, 4.60 and 2.95 times higher than GQDs (2.31 μmol g-1 h- 1), BNNR (7.27 μmol g-1 h- 1) and C-BNNR (11.34 μmol g-1 h- 1), respectively. And the in situ FT-IR was used to determine the pathway of CO. Meanwhile, GQDs/C-BNNR possess certain CO2 photocatalytic reduction ability in the NIR region due to the upconversion ability of GQDs. In conclusion, we believe that this work further explores the research of porous BN in CO2 photocatalysis and provides new ideas for the design of clean and green novel metal-free photocatalysts.
